questions.question_text: The Consumer/Licensee Relationship disclosure form informs a consumer about agency options. Which document actually establishes an agency relationship?
questions.correct_answer: An agency agreement
distractors: The signed disclosure form itself
A purchase and sale agreement
A mortgage commitment letter
questions.explanation: The disclosure form explains the agency options available but does not create an agency relationship — it expressly states it is not a contract. Only an agency agreement (such as a listing or buyer agency agreement) establishes the relationship.

questions.question_text: A listing agent hosts an open house. Under Massachusetts regulation, what must the agent do about agency disclosure for walk-in visitors?
questions.correct_answer: Conspicuously disclose any pre-existing agency relationship by sign, poster, or distributed listing literature
distractors: Hand each visitor the written Licensee-Consumer Relationship Disclosure form when they enter
Obtain each visitor's signature on a dual agency consent
Nothing — open houses are fully exempt from all agency disclosure
questions.explanation: 254 CMR 3.00(13) exempts open houses from the written disclosure form: the licensee must instead conspicuously disclose any pre-existing agency relationship by sign, poster, distributed listing literature, or property description form. If listing literature is the disclosure vehicle, the agency disclosure in it must be more conspicuous than any other written material — and the written form is still due at any later first personal meeting, such as a follow-up private showing.

questions.question_text: Why must the Consumer/Licensee Relationship disclosure be provided before any confidential information is shared?
questions.correct_answer: So the consumer understands who the agent represents
distractors: To comply with federal RESPA disclosure requirements
To establish the agreed-upon commission rate
To transfer liability from the agent to the consumer
questions.explanation: The disclosure must come first so the consumer understands the agency relationship before sharing sensitive information. Without this knowledge, a consumer might unknowingly reveal information to the wrong party's agent.

questions.question_text: A licensee meets a potential buyer at a coffee shop to discuss a specific property. The licensee forgets the disclosure form. The licensee should:
questions.correct_answer: Delay the property discussion until the form is provided
distractors: Proceed and provide the form at the next meeting
Verbally explain the agency options instead
Email the form after the meeting ends
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be provided at the first personal meeting about a specific property, before confidential information is shared. The licensee should delay substantive discussion.

questions.question_text: The Consumer/Licensee Relationship disclosure is required by which Massachusetts regulation?
questions.correct_answer: Board of Registration regulations
distractors: Chapter 93A consumer protection
Chapter 151B fair housing
Title 5 environmental rules
questions.explanation: The Consumer/Licensee Relationship disclosure is mandated by the Board of Registration's regulations governing licensee conduct. It is specific to real estate licensing, not consumer protection or fair housing law.

questions.question_text: A licensee owns a title company and refers all clients there without disclosing the ownership. This is a violation of:
questions.correct_answer: Conflict of interest disclosure rules
distractors: Advertising regulations for licensees
Trust account handling requirements
Fair housing statutory provisions
questions.explanation: Licensees must disclose any personal interest in a transaction, including ownership of companies to which they refer clients. Undisclosed referrals to owned businesses are conflict of interest violations.

questions.question_text: A mortgage lender offers a licensee a referral fee for sending homebuyer clients its way. The licensee must:
questions.correct_answer: Decline the fee — referral fees for mortgage business are prohibited
distractors: Accept it after disclosing it to the clients
Accept it if the fee is under a nominal amount
Split the fee with their employing broker
questions.explanation: Under RESPA Section 8, giving or accepting a fee or kickback for referring settlement-service business — including mortgage loans — is prohibited, and disclosure does not cure the violation. Financial relationships that ARE permitted (such as an ownership interest in an affiliated business) must be disclosed to clients.

questions.question_text: A listing agent is also the property owner but does not disclose this to buyers. This violates:
questions.correct_answer: The duty to disclose personal interest
distractors: Only federal RESPA disclosure regulations
Only the Statute of Frauds requirements
Only Board advertising rules for licensees
questions.explanation: A licensee who is also the property owner has a significant personal interest that must be disclosed. Failure to disclose ownership is a serious conflict of interest violation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following relationships must a licensee disclose to clients?
questions.correct_answer: Financial ties to recommended service providers
distractors: Personal friendships with other agents
Past transactions with the seller
Their political party affiliation
questions.explanation: Financial relationships with inspectors, lenders, or other service providers to whom the licensee refers clients must be disclosed. Personal, political, and past transaction details need not be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent recommends three home inspectors, one of whom is the agent's cousin. The agent must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the family relationship
distractors: Only recommend unrelated inspectors
Not provide any inspector recommendations
Get Board approval for the referral
questions.explanation: The agent must disclose the family relationship so the buyer can make an informed decision. Licensees must not recommend inspectors in a way that creates an undisclosed conflict of interest.

questions.question_text: Under the Massachusetts rental fee reform effective August 1, 2025, who must pay the rental broker's fee?
questions.correct_answer: The party who hired the broker
distractors: Always the tenant regardless of hiring
Always the landlord of the property
The fee is split equally between both
questions.explanation: Under the 2025 rental fee reform, the broker's fee must be paid by the party who hired the broker. Landlords can no longer require tenants to pay the landlord's broker fee.

questions.question_text: A landlord hires a broker to find a tenant. The landlord then tells the tenant to pay the broker fee. Under the new law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited — the landlord must pay
distractors: Permitted if in the lease agreement
Allowed if the tenant agrees in writing
Legal for commercial leases only
questions.explanation: Under the August 2025 rental fee reform, landlords cannot require tenants to pay the landlord's broker fee — the party who hired the broker pays. A violating landlord faces liability under Chapter 93A, including up to treble damages and attorney fees.

questions.question_text: A tenant independently hires a broker to help find an apartment. Can the broker charge the tenant a fee?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the broker works exclusively for the tenant
distractors: No, tenants can never be charged a broker fee
Yes, for any amount the broker chooses to charge
Only if the landlord also agrees to pay a portion
questions.explanation: A broker may charge a tenant only if the tenant independently engaged the broker and the broker worked exclusively on the tenant's behalf in negotiations. This is the only way a tenant can be charged.

questions.question_text: At the first personal meeting with a prospective tenant, a rental broker must present:
questions.correct_answer: A fee disclosure form
distractors: A lead paint disclosure
A lease summary document
A building code certificate
questions.explanation: Under 254 CMR 7.00, a rental broker must give a prospective tenant a written fee disclosure at their first personal meeting, stating whether the tenant will pay a fee, the amount, and when it is due. The 2025 fee reform reinforces this transparency: the fee may only be charged to the party who first engaged the broker.

questions.question_text: A landlord improperly charges a tenant $3,000 for the landlord's broker fee. Under the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (Chapter 93A), the tenant may recover up to:
questions.correct_answer: $9,000 plus attorney fees
distractors: $3,000 only
$6,000 maximum
$3,000 plus a $500 penalty
questions.explanation: The broker-fee statute itself contains no damages clause; tenants sue under Chapter 93A, which allows up to treble damages for willful or knowing violations ($3,000 x 3 = $9,000) plus reasonable attorney fees.

questions.question_text: Massachusetts home inspectors must be licensed by which entity?
questions.correct_answer: Board of Registration of Home Inspectors
distractors: Board of Registration of Real Estate
Division of Professional Engineers
Department of Public Safety
questions.explanation: Massachusetts licenses home inspectors separately through the Board of Registration of Home Inspectors, which is distinct from the Board of Registration of Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons.

questions.question_text: Under Massachusetts home-inspection regulations effective October 15, 2025 (implementing the Affordable Homes Act), a seller accepts an offer only if the buyer waives the home inspection. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited under the new law
distractors: Permitted with a written waiver
Legal if the buyer initiates the waiver
Allowed for properties sold as-is
questions.explanation: 760 CMR 74.00, adopted under the 2024 Affordable Homes Act and effective October 15, 2025, prohibits sellers from conditioning acceptance of an offer on the buyer waiving a home inspection. A buyer may still voluntarily decline an inspection after the offer is accepted.

questions.question_text: A seller receives two offers. One includes a home inspection contingency and the other waives it. Under current Massachusetts law, the seller should:
questions.correct_answer: Evaluate offers on overall merits, not penalize inspection requests
distractors: Always accept the offer that waives the home inspection contingency
Require both buyers to waive their home inspection contingency
Report both competing offers to the Board of Registration
questions.explanation: Under regulations implementing the Affordable Homes Act, sellers cannot condition acceptance on waiver of inspection rights — and may not accept an offer when told in advance that the buyer intends to waive the inspection. Sellers should evaluate offers on their overall merits, not penalize buyers who want an inspection.

questions.question_text: A real estate licensee has a duty to inform buyers about their right to:
questions.correct_answer: A home inspection
distractors: A free appraisal
A property tax abatement
A mold inspection at seller's cost
questions.explanation: Licensees have a responsibility to inform buyers about their right to a home inspection. This duty is now reinforced by the Affordable Homes Act's protection of inspection rights.

questions.question_text: A listing agent suggests that the seller only consider offers that waive the home inspection contingency. Under current law, the agent is:
questions.correct_answer: Advising a violation of the Affordable Homes Act
distractors: Giving standard market advice to the seller
Acting within the scope of the seller's best interest
Offering a permissible and common negotiation strategy
questions.explanation: Since October 2025, sellers cannot condition offer acceptance on buyers waiving inspection rights. Advising a seller to do so would be advising a violation of the Affordable Homes Act.
